original_abstract: This tutorial review examines the design, synthesis, and host–guest chemistry of discrete coordination cages formed through the self-assembly of banana-shaped, bis-monodentate pyridyl ligands and square-planar metal cations, specifically Pd(II) and Pt(II). While [M₂L₄] stoichiometry represents the most common structural motif for these assemblies, the review explores how variations in ligand geometry, flexibility, and environmental factors can lead to more complex architectures, including [M₃L₆] and [M₄L₈] rings, intricate double-trefoil knots, and interpenetrated double-cages. Because these structures are typically cationic, they exhibit a high affinity for anionic guests, often demonstrating significant selectivity based on the size and shape of the internal cavities. Detailed discussions are provided on advanced host–guest phenomena such as the linear stacking of metal complexes, positive cooperativity in allosteric binding, and the development of stimuli-responsive systems. Finally, the review highlights the integration of functional, redox-active, or photoswitchable backbones into the ligand design, facilitating the light-controlled uptake and release of molecular cargo and paving the way for applications in molecular machinery and artificial catalysis.
humanized_abstract: This tutorial review provides an overview of the design, synthesis and host–guest chemistry of discrete coordination cages built from simple banana-shaped, bis-monodentate pyridyl ligands and square-planar Pd(II) and Pt(II) metal centers, resulting in [M₂L₄] specie(s). While the four-ringed configuration constitutes the predominant structure for these type of self-assembled coordination cages, the influence of differences in the shapes, sizes and flexibilities of the banana-shaped pyridyl ligands, as well as on solution conditions may afford a range of additional topologies for these cages such as [M₃L₆] rings, double-trefoil knots and more rarely interpenetrated double-cages. Regardless of topologies, these cages always operate as selective hosts towards anionic guests within their cationic cavities and we provide a thorough discussion of many of the sophisticated host–guest (HXG) properties realized with these cages such as for example metallation-dependent stacking of metal-centres or the evidence for allosteric positive cooperativity or even most frequently stimuli-controlled (HXG) assemblies. Furthermore, with respect to potential applications in the field of molecular machinery and artificial catalysis we address some new aspects including the grafting of catalytic sites on the ligand backbone, incorporating reversible redox switches and even photo-switches to modulate the opening and closing of the cages with certain metal centers allowing for repetitive cycles of guest uptake and release under successive irradiations and in solution or solid state.
